reviews.question: What knowledge, methods, skills, insights, or ways of thinking did you develop in this course? Please describe three specific things you learned.
responses: Basic python skills, problem solving, computer science fundamentals

In this class I developed a strong foundation in Python programming and learned how to think about problems in a clear and structured way. I gained confidence in writing code from scratch including using loops, conditionals, functions, and list and dictionary comprehension. I also learned how to work with external files such as reading and processing text files, extracting information, and turning raw data into something meaningful. Finally, I learned how to break down complex problems into manageable parts and design algorithms that allowed me to approach each assignment with more independence and creativity.
I learned the basics of how to code in python. I also learned how to debug a code that I made. I also learned the seven steps to creating a successful code on the first go.

I learned how to code something for the first time in Python. Specifically, I learned how to problem-solve and piece apart a prompt into smaller pieces that can be addressed with different tools in different ways. For example, utilising a for loop alongside an if statement allows me to set specific conditions for the particular outcome I desire to receive.
I learned the basic level of Python, problem-solving methods, and different ways to organize data.
This course served as a tutorial to demonstrate the basics of how to use python, variables, for loops, if statements, among other techniques to jump start one's journey in coding.
I learned how to code in Python, solving problems by writing functions with if statements, loops, and dictionaries.

CS 101 taught me how to break complex problems into smaller, manageable steps before writing any code. Assignments like GuessWord and RecommenderEngine forced me to think in terms of helper functions, logical flow, and test cases—skills that changed how I approach challenges in any subject. I learned to think carefully about inputs, outputs, edge cases, and the exact behavior a function should have. Through hands-on practice, I developed fluency with loops, conditionals, dictionaries, lists, recursion, and object-oriented concepts. I also learned how to read error messages, fix bugs systematically, and write clean, readable code.
I learned 1) how to read code 2) how to problem solve creatively 3) how to learn with AI as a resource when studying
I have learned basic coding skills and some underlying logic.
I learned to write in a very specific way. And I learned there are so many ways to be wrong.
- Working with python language - Debugging my code when a billion error bars pop up - Using different methods, approaches to code writing
Learned how to code, debug, and how to use while loops.

1) I think this course really built my ability to think computationally. Being able to clearly break problems down into smaller chunks that can be implemented by the computer is a skillset developed in this course. 2) Due to the many due dates of assignments in this class, the course taught me how to stay on top of my workload, manage my time, and keep track of deadlines. 3) I feel as though this course also helped me be a more creative problem solver. Lots of the class focused on coming up with clever algorithms to efficiently solve a variety of problems.
How to fix bugs, how to think through complex problems, how to make code more efficient in the way of using less lines of code

I developed my problem-solving skills and learned the basics of coding. I learned about strings, lists, dictionaries, tuples, sorting, etc. While doing my assignments, I improved my debugging capabilities.
1. How to break a large problem down into smaller problems. 2. How to debug code effectively. 3. How Python works.

- Problem solving through the 7 steps (focusing mostly on the first 4 steps by working out an example of the problem first) - Learning to effectively debug - Applying CS concepts to understand the logic behind real world systems (ex. recommender system)
We learned how to use python to code assignments, we learned how to apply coding skills to problems, we learned how to take a real life problem and use code to solve it.
Logical thinking, problem solving
Learned basic python coding. Learned how to debug and rewrite code.
I learned to code.
Learning how to think analytically through the language of code, breaking down larger complex projects and chunks into much smaller pieces, creating visualizations and imagery via python
I learned the basics for coding in Python such as for loops, while loops, and conditional lines of code.
coding
I already had a small coding background coming in and this course helped refine it. I learned more about looping, was introduced to sorting, and learned about dictionaries
Coding and the logic behind it.
New python functions and ways to use it.
How to code in python, problem-solving, applying skills to real-world problems
Python basics, coding methods/problem-solving methods, exposure to applications of comp sci

We learned how to use loops to iterate through and use data, we learned how to store data in structures like lists, dictionaries, and tuples, and we learned how to apply these skills to solve problems in apts
Teaching myself Reading off slides Being creative.
I developed Python coding skills. I learned to read and write lines of code and solve problems using code. It thus also enhanced my problem-solving and analytical skills.
I learned how to code in python, how to problem-solve better, and how to approach a scenario different ways.
I learned how to code in python, I developed better problem-solving skills, and because of this class I might minor in CS.
7 steps of programming, introduction to python, and problem solving and critical thinking.
I learned how to use PyCharm, take a multi-step approach to problem-solving (shout-out to APTs!!!), and how to debug code.
Good understanding of basic Python concepts.
I learned a lot more about python.
I learned how to use the 7 steps, how to navigate and use the Python programming console, and I learned how to study for a topic very different than my conventional topics in the past.

I developed a lot of python knowledge and how to go about creating and understanding code. I learned the 7 steps of code which helped me understand future assignments. I also learned more about how Duke courses work and what is expected.

I learned how to code in Python with concepts such as variables, lists, dictionaries, sorting, and functions. I learned how to code these from scratch and how they can interact and be used together to solve problems.
The if logic, prioritizing, and
How to break problems into smaller steps to solve them, using for loops and while loops, using set methods, using dictionaries to solve large problems
I learned a lot about the fundamentals of Python and how to logistically solve problems in a short period of time.
n/a, i learned mostly through the book and having chatgpt explain concepts
Simply problem-solving. I built that skill from the APTs and Assignments.

I learned how to apply methods of coding to real-life scenarios. I also learned that though there may be multiple ways to approach an objective, there are some that are more efficient than others. I also learned time-management skills.

reviews.question: Reflecting on the overall learning environment of this class, in what ways did the instructor(s) and the structure or components of the course facilitate your learning? Are there specific course components or methods of instruction you’d keep for future years?
responses: I think the assignments were generally good, and the deadlines were realistic. The lectures were usually informative.

The instructor created a very supportive and encouraging learning environment and explained concepts with clarity and patience. They often broke down difficult topics into simple steps which made the material more accessible even for students who were new to coding. The structure of weekly assignments, practice problems, and examples shown in class helped reinforce what we learned and made the material feel less intimidating. The live coding demonstrations and the instructor’s willingness to pause for questions were especially helpful, and these are components I would keep in future years.
I liked the WOTOs, as they allowed me to think for myself and compare with a peer and hear their thinking process.
I felt pretty engaged during the majority of lectures, as we often have to do WOTOs, and Professor Rodger does a good job of asking us for input to answer the questions we're looking at in class.
I like how the course materials are organized into dates.
I think that the WOTOs during class were helpful in keeping students engaged.
Readings and the quizzes before the lecture were helpful in preparation for the class.

The course was structured in a way that made learning incremental and achievable. Lectures gave me conceptual clarity, while labs and assignments gave me immediate practice applying those concepts. I felt the clear assignment specifications, office hours, and the use of starter code was very helpful throughout the semester. The environment felt supportive and designed for growth, even for students with little prior programming experience.
I think everyone collaborates well.
I enjoyed apts and assignments. I also found the labs fun.

The APTs and assignment really kept me busy with this course. The APTs are very helpful to understand what we are learning, and practice what we are learning. Assignments are harder but helps me feel like I've actually produced something.
It was not a fun intro course.

Overall, the class had a lot of support for students and was extremely well-organized. I found the course website to be easy to navigate once I got acquainted with it, and it helped make the course more manageable. There were plenty of office hours and times available to meet with professors or TAs should I have had questions about the course outside of class.

The APTs and assignments were very useful in helping utilize the material learned. I would also maintain the lecture style but maybe with more examples of how to implement the concepts we use in class.

The instructor facilitated my learning by answering questions in class, seeking participaction, and being available when needing help. Runestone and APTs are the components that I would keep for future years.
The slides and recordings were super helpful.
The instructor made sure to always include us by asking questions and doing fun games which I really liked.
In class mini quizzes on topics discussed in class
Good leaarning environment. The Wotos help by working together with classmates to solve problems.
It was a very great course, and I learned a lot of Python. However it is painfully slow and beginner level, so if you have any previous experience then take 201.
Using in-class questions to facilitate engagement in a large lecture course
Make the assignment descriptions shorter
it was helpful in class to go through the code
I liked the APTs
Consistent, low-pressure means of keeping engagement through praire-learn and WOTOs.
She gave past tests to help us study.
Wotos, Labs, providing practice
benefited from the ta review sessions before each test
lectures were beneficial and to the point when teaching new content
In a lecture hall using slides, probably should not just read off slides because that is what the professor does.
Stuff that worked for me were APTs, labs, WOTOs, assignments and just the access to old exams and tests.
The course structure was very organized, but class should cover harder concepts that are on tests/assignments.

It was a lecture with a slideshow, but also interactive elements as a participation game. These helped for staying focused during the lecture. The review game as the lab before an exam was also very helpful.
Practice exams were super useful, APTs were also really helpful as they aligned with the exams well.
I found the APTs very valuable to my learning. Also, going through APT examples during lecture (even if to get it started) was helpful. I also thought labs were relevant and engaging.

In class WOTOs/mini quizzes were helpful to keep me engaged. Doing prep before the lecture/reading the textbook was always helpful since lecture was often on the boring side and would have trouble focusing.
I would keep the lab reviews for sure.

The repetition with concepts inside and outside of the lecture, in various formats, such as the diversity between APTs, Assignments, and reading quizzes. I think the time between submission of the Assignments and APTs could be longer/ the time to complete certain projects could be lengthened after the assignment is officially posted. A few times it felt like we had less than a week to complete a longer assignment, which are key to the class and shouldn't be rushed.

The professor’s structure of the course helped entirely because of the course website which made everything clear. This website should definitely be kept. As well as the jeopardy review labs before exams should be kept as well. These two things helped ensure success in the course.

I liked how we did examples in class as well as how the APT assignments could be submitted as many times as it took to get right. Being able to test out different ways of coding something to see what actually works helps me to fully understand how to use each function and item type for future scenarios.
Professor Rodgers set clear expectations and included a variety of practice materials; the old exams were especially helpful for test prep. The extensions were also very useful.
I think that solving APTs in class was helpful.

The relatable and creative APTs and Assignments motivated me to spend more time on them as I had an enjoyable time solving them, such as the Assignment on creating a word game. I would definitely keep it going forward.
i missed most classes because it was too early

Well, I personally saw the class as more independent since we had such a huge class in a theater. The instructor helped me only by assigning the work and from that I learned the material using the material they provided. and the internet.
The Peer instruction activities were helpful and should be kept.

reviews.question: The course was intellectually stimulating. It made me think in new ways, encouraged me to adopt different points of view, or challenged me to develop new skills.
What made this class stimulating or how could it be more intellectually stimulating if it wasn’t?
responses: This class was intellectually stimulating because it constantly pushed me to think differently and translate ideas into precise instructions for a computer. Learning how to debug, trace errors, and understand why code behaves a certain way made me think logically and carefully. The moments when a difficult problem finally worked were especially motivating. The class could be even more stimulating by incorporating more real datasets or short in class challenges that require creative problem solving.
I think the WOTOS were the most stimulating part of the class. Maybe find a way to make the lectures more interactive or interesting?
I never programmed before this class, so I was only learning.
This class fosters problem-solving skills.

Finding another way to present class information that is a bit more engaging for students, I think would prove helpful. The class is also around 100 people or so, and I don't think Bryan Center - a lecture hall that seats 500 - to be as conducive to learning. Everyone was way to spread out from each other.
APTs were stimulating my problem-solving skills.

It was stimulating because it pushed me to think differently, not just about coding, but about logical reasoning, structure, and clarity. There’s something uniquely rewarding about adjusting a few lines of code and watching an entire function suddenly work. To make it even more stimulating, the course could occasionally introduce real-world mini-applications (small examples of where the concepts we’re learning show up in fields like data science, finance, biology, etc).
I think the instructor is passionate and UTAs are helpful.
It was super hard I had no choice but to be stimulated.
The assignments are always super different and hard! The textbook is super engaging and helpful towards my understanding of coding.
The ta's.

I liked the problem-solving aspect of this course (often found in APTs and Assignments) as I enjoyed thinking creatively about how to solve problems and working to implement a program that successfully translated my algorithm to functional code.
The rigor of the assignments
There were countless topics and details we learned about when it came to proper Python coding.
I liked all the assignments, they were really fun applications of the topics we learned in class.
WOTOs made the class intellectually stimulating.
It was very stimulating because we had to learn coding and things we never learned before.
I think the logical/problem solving nature of the is how I naturally think which I found stimulating.
The content itself is interesting and once you get the code working it feels satisfying.
n/a
Better resources for learning outside of the class
High level of engagement
Having to think through each individual step of every single thing I coded was thought provoking
Always connected what we were learning to the real world and people who pioneered the field.
More practice that is like the exam week-to-week
better explanations in lectures
the problem-solving aspects of the assignments pushed me to think of ways to solve problems with the methods and structures we learned in lectures
It could be more stimulating with less busy work.
Assignments and APTs made this class extremely stimulating as it allowed me to apply my coding skills to solve real-life problems.

I think that harder concepts weren't really taught in class and moreso self-taught when studying. Class lecture was not very engaging. Also, the assignments were not very related a lot of the time and just tedious/frustrating.
Most of coding is problem solving and that is what we needed to do for this class which is quite intellectually stimulating.
All the assignments and APTs were very stimulating as you were problem solving and figuring out why your code doesn't work every week.
It was because it challenged me to apply real-life implementations of code- like the Recommender Assignment!
Very much what you put in you get out. As a result, it can be a toss up if it is engaging.
This class was stimulating because I kept on learning new things in python.

The lectures could be more diversified and engaging, like when we had the in-class sorting demonstration with the playing cards. Additionally, having our computers open all the time to complete the WOTOS, (largely because it disconnects you if you close your computer) makes the classes overall attention very low.
The content made the class very stimulating and the way it was taught was great.
I thought it was stimulating.

The community between students was very nice, but to stimulate learning I would suggest condensing the number of assignments, improving their clarity and instructions, lectures were okay, and letting lab time also not be as rushed.
The assignments were very diverse/interesting.
The leetcode style questions on tests and APTs were very helpful in stimulating me.
it could've been more intellectually stimulating if our work was more creative instead of restricted during assignments
It is good as it is. Maybe just more engagement from the teacher with the class.
The various assignments we were assigned helped to make sure I understood the concepts we learned and was able to apply them in a variety of ways and situations.

reviews.question: Considering all components of the course (lectures, discussions, sections/labs, assessments, projects, course environment, etc), overall the course was
Based on the effectiveness of instruction (clarity, expertise, enthusiasm, rigor, support, inspiration, etc), overall the instructor, Instructor(s), was
What would you like to say about this course to a student who is considering taking it in the future?
responses: If you have no computer science experience, this would be a great one to take.
If you have zero prior coding experience, it's absolutely possible to get a high A in the course!

I would tell future students that this course is a great introduction to coding even if they have little or no prior experience. The instructor explains everything clearly and gives many examples that make the material feel approachable. The class moves at a manageable pace, and the assignments help you build your skills step by step. If you stay engaged and practice consistently, you will finish the course feeling more confident in your ability to code and solve problems logically.
If you want to learn computer science definitely take this course, it's a nice introduction.
Don't be afraid to seek support and ask questions when necessary.
It is a rewarding, challenging, and fun class.
You definitely need to designate your own time to master the material. I think this course is very self-structured and you get out of it what you put into it.
The class has a lot of homework.

This is an excellent introduction to computer science, even if you’ve never written a line of code before. The course builds your skills gradually, the support system is strong, and the assignments are challenging in a rewarding way. You’ll finish the semester not only knowing how to code, but understanding how to think more clearly and logically. If you engage with the assignments and ask questions, you’ll grow a lot, both as a programmer and as a problem-solver.
Take it without hesitation.
Go to every class never fall behind or you might be cooked.
Be prepared for the time commitment, its not hard work but just busy work if you pay attention to what we are learning!
It is a lot and you do need experience even if It says you don't.

I would recommend this course to students wondering if they might enjoy computer science who might not have a lot of previous coding experience. I feel that it is a good introduction to the fundamentals of programming, and I was able to get the hang of coding fairly quickly despite never having done so before. I would warn that this class does require quite a bit of time outside of lectures due to the several assignments given each week.
Be prepared to spend a lot of time on assignments and APTs
Do not fall behind because it is incredibly difficult to catch up since topics build on one another.
Stay on track of everything, it builds up pretty quickly.
It will definitely challenge you and you need to put in the work to understand the material and learn how to code on paper not just on your computer.
Do not take other heavy workload classes at the same time as this takes a lot of time.
If you do not have any coding background and want to ease your way into coding or learn a few things than this is a good class.
Take it if you have no coding experience. It's a very easy course and very helpful for learning. But don't take if you have any coding experience in Java or Python.
If it is for you, then it'll be easy
its a great class for intro to coding if you have never coded before
Just study for exams
Good skills can come from this course.
This class is not too difficult, properly preparing for exams and engaging with the content of the class will ensure success.
Don't take it just take 201 if you're trying to be a cs major.
It is a very organized course, so there's a lot of support!
As long as you come to the lecture and pay attention the class is not too hard to understand. It is very much worth taking.

Just because it's a 101 class does not symbolize that it's going to be easy. You have to dedicate a lot of time and you will reap the benefits as you'll gain such a strong foundation in computer science later on.
Go to lectures!
Good if you want to learn basic programming skills and have no coding experience. If you have coding experience, you can just go to 201.
Do the work and at least be somewhat engaged in class.
This class is a great start for programming and learning how to code. If you are interested in these topics I recommend it!

Take it! It really gets you to understand whether or not you want to move further with coding and computer science. Professor Rodger is great and the course is super light and interesting. There is so much help and it almost becomes fun to do.
Definitely use the past exams to study, and submit APTs and Assignments as many times as you need to check as you go.
I would say that as most Duke 101 courses, it is a weed course that is not very well designed for students who have not coded at all in the past.
It's alot of work and you need to keep up with it. It's easy to get behind
I would 100% recommend to take CS 101 before 201 because it provides a completely different angle to the CS degree.
skip it and go to 201 directly. learn python urself on khan academy in a week if you have to.
It is a basic and fun course. Just don't fall behind.
There are a lot of assignments but you will definitely end up with a good understanding of basic Python programming.

reviews.question: How many hours in a typical week did you spend on this course (outside of class meetings)?
